one_line_takeaway: For LLM-generated training/fine-tuning datasets, simple lexical (green-list) watermarks degrade badly under downstream fine-tuning and short-text generation, while a stealthy grammatical/steganographic watermark (forcing present-continuous tense or passive voice) achieves perfect detection (WSR=1.000) with minimal utility loss and far lower generation cost, though all watermark types remain removable via adversarial fine-tuning of the downstream model.
key_finding: Input-level watermarks were highly robust in the non-adversarial setting: WSR exceeded 0.900 in essentially all ⟨upstream, downstream, dataset⟩ combinations, reaching 1.000 for nearly all Llama-generated data, with the lowest reported value 0.920 for ⟨Ministral,BERT,AG News⟩ under the stylistic method; clean/real accuracy (CTS/Real CTS) showed no meaningful degradation versus baseline (e.g. Real CTS=0.976 and CTS=0.982 for the traditional method on ⟨Llama,BERT,DBpedia⟩). Output-level results diverged sharply by method: the weak (green-list logit-bias) watermark degraded substantially after passing through downstream fine-tuning -- Z-Score dropped from an initial ~20.000 in the training data to a best downstream case of 7.317 for ⟨Llama,T5,AG News⟩, and collapsed to near/below the WSR-relevant threshold on the shorter-text DialogSum task (average Z-Score ~1.0-1.2 or negative for several downstream models), making it length-dependent and unreliable for short generations. The robust (narrow fixed-vocabulary) watermark achieved WSR up to 1.000 (e.g. ⟨Llama,Qwen,AG News⟩) but at a documented utility cost (e.g. MAUVE dropped from 0.640 to 0.395 and PPL rose from 13.603 to 16.814 for the T5/Qwen AG News case versus the weak watermark) and the authors note the injected tokens (e.g. 'ikun', 'personne2') are conspicuous to a human inspector, leading them to conclude it is 'not an effective watermark approach.' The steganographic (grammatical, present-continuous/passive-voice) watermark achieved perfect WSR=1.000 across all tested combinations in Tables 4 and 5 with the least utility impact and the fastest generation time (0.7s/sample vs. 7s for robust and 310s for weak), which the authors identify as the best-performing output-level method. Watermark removal experiments show both fine-tuning and (to a lesser, more variable extent) pruning/quantization can reduce WSR/Z-Score, with fine-tuning being most effective at stripping watermarks but sometimes destabilizing downstream utility (e.g. the robust watermark's MAUVE for ⟨Ministral,Qwen,AG News⟩ dropped from 0.631 to 0.605 while PPL rose from 18.775 to 27.970 after adversarial fine-tuning).
core_contribution: The paper proposes watermarking schemes for LLM-generated DATASETS (not just LLM outputs), designed so an LLM owner ('defender') can later detect whether a downstream model was trained on data the LLM generated for a user, even though the defender never observes the user's downstream training pipeline. It splits downstream use into two categories and gives distinct watermark mechanisms for each: (1) Input-level (data used to train a classifier): a 'traditional' trigger-based watermark (a backdoor-like trigger word/phrase injected via the system prompt for samples of a target class, Algorithm 1) and a 'stylistic' watermark that reformulates the target-class text into three-line poetry as a stealthier trigger (Section 3.2, building on Pan et al.'s style-trigger idea but without the auxiliary classifier module Pan et al. used, since the defender cannot intervene in downstream training). (2) Output-level (LLM outputs used as generation targets, e.g. for fine-tuning): three methods -- a 'weak' watermark using green/red-list logit biasing with Z-Score detection (Algorithm 2, adapting Kirchenbauer-style watermarking), a 'robust' watermark that narrows the green list to a small fixed vocabulary (e.g., forcing generation of specific tokens like 'ikun' or the French 'personne2') and verifies by direct token detection instead of Z-Score (Algorithm 3), and a 'steganographic' watermark that encodes the signal via syntactic transformations (forcing present continuous tense or passive voice) rather than word choice (Algorithm 4).
threat_model: Two parties: an adversary (a downstream model trainer) who queries the upstream/victim LLM to generate a dataset cheaply and then fine-tunes their own model on it, and a defender (the LLM owner) who wants to later determine whether a given downstream model was trained on LLM-generated data, in order to protect copyright/IP. Explicit assumptions: the defender has full white-box access to their own upstream LLM (can set system prompts, adjust logit bias) but has NO access to the adversary's inputs, no access to the downstream training process, and cannot build a shadow/membership-inference dataset because different generation hyperparameters cause large distributional variation. This rules out membership inference as a detection strategy. The adversary's motivation is cost reduction (avoiding manual dataset curation, e.g. vs. AG News/DBpedia/IMDb-style curation effort); the defender's motivation is copyright/IP protection of the upstream LLM's generative capability, not end-user privacy or model safety.
limitations: The authors themselves note: (1) the weak watermark's effectiveness is strongly dependent on generated text length, trading watermark strength against generation cost (310s/sample) and downstream utility -- a 'fundamental limitation'; (2) the robust watermark, despite near-perfect WSR, is explicitly judged by the authors as not a viable watermark because injected fixed tokens are easily human-detectable and degrade text quality; (3) the stylistic (poetry) watermark's effectiveness is lower for Ministral-generated data (e.g. 0.920 WSR) because the paper's setting cannot use Pan et al.'s auxiliary classifier module to reinforce watermark learning during downstream training, since the defender has no access to that training process; (4) fine-tuning is confirmed as an effective watermark-removal attack for both input- and output-level watermarks, though the authors argue this is impractical for adversaries motivated by cost savings, since fine-tuning on real data reintroduces the costs the adversary sought to avoid; (5) the paper's own text notes the weak-watermark implementation deliberately departs from prior green-list schemes (does not condition on input tokens) for vLLM inference-speed reasons, which they acknowledge yields slightly lower scores than the original approach.

quantitative_results: Input-level WSR exceeds 0.900 across nearly all ⟨upstream,downstream,dataset⟩ combos; lowest value 0.920 for ⟨Ministral,BERT,AG News⟩ stylistic watermark
Weak watermark: training-set Z-Score ~20.000, best downstream Z-Score 7.317 for ⟨Llama,T5,AG News⟩ vs. threshold 4.000; DialogSum downstream Z-Score falls to ~1.0-1.2 or negative for several models
Robust watermark WSR up to 1.000 (e.g. ⟨Llama,Qwen,AG News⟩); token vocabulary constrained to 50 tokens
Robust vs weak utility for ⟨Llama,Qwen,T5⟩: MAUVE 0.395 vs 0.640, PPL 16.814 vs 13.603
Steganographic (present continuous & passive voice) watermark: WSR=1.000 in all tested combinations (Tables 4 & 5)
Generation time per sample: weak watermark ~310s, robust watermark ~7s, steganographic watermark ~0.7s
Adversarial fine-tuning on robust watermark ⟨Ministral,Qwen,AG News⟩: MAUVE drops 0.631→0.605, PPL rises 18.775→27.970
Baseline (no watermark) output-level Z-Scores near zero (e.g. -0.074 to 1.074) and WSR(W)/WSR(R) near 0.000, confirming no false-positive watermark detection
baselines_compared: Clean/unwatermarked LLM-generated text baselines (Traditional Baseline, Stylistic Baseline in Figures 1-3)
Kirchenbauer et al. green-red list watermarking (weak watermark method)
Pan et al. stylistic/style-trigger backdoor watermarking (adapted without its auxiliary classifier)
